lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: What does the guide RNA do in the CRISPR-Cas9 system?
choices: It cuts the DNA strands directly
It tells Cas9 which DNA sequence to cut
It repairs the DNA after cutting
It builds new proteins from scratch
why: The guide RNA carries a ~20-letter sequence that matches the target DNA — it's the address. Cas9 is the enzyme that does the actual cutting once the guide leads it to the spot.

lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: What did this 2012 paper actually demonstrate?
choices: Curing a genetic disease in a patient
Editing genes inside living human beings
Programmable, targeted DNA cutting in a test tube
That CRISPR exists in bacteria for the first time
why: The study showed Cas9 could be reprogrammed with a custom guide to cut chosen DNA sequences in a tube. Working inside living cells and treating patients were later advances built on this result.

lessonTitle: The paper that turned a bacterial defense into gene-editing scissors
q: Why was this a leap over earlier gene-editing methods?
choices: It required no DNA at all
You reprogram the target by writing a short RNA instead of engineering a new protein each time
It was the first method that could cut DNA
It eliminated all off-target mistakes
why: Earlier approaches needed a custom protein built for every target — slow and expensive. CRISPR-Cas9 lets you retarget by ordering a short, cheap guide RNA. Off-target cuts remained a real challenge to solve later.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: In a typical prospect-theory gamble, people offered a sure $500 versus a coin-flip for $1,000 or nothing tend to do what?
choices: Take the gamble, because it could pay double
Take the sure $500, playing it safe with gains
Refuse to answer either way
Split evenly — the model predicts indifference
why: Both options average $500, so a pure calculator would be indifferent. But for gains, real people are risk-averse and grab the sure thing — one of the clean breaks from the rational model.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: What does 'loss aversion' say about how a $100 loss compares to a $100 gain?
choices: They feel exactly equal in size
The gain feels stronger because we're optimists
The loss hurts roughly twice as much as the gain feels good
Neither registers unless the amount is large
why: Kahneman and Tversky found losses 'loom larger' than gains, with the pain of a loss running about twice the pleasure of an equal gain — a ratio later estimated near 2.

lessonTitle: Why a loss hurts twice as much as a win feels good
q: Why can the same final dollar amount feel like a gain to one person and a loss to another?
choices: Because of differences in expected utility
Because they have different reference points
Because probabilities are subjective
Because one of them did the math wrong
why: Prospect theory says we judge outcomes relative to a reference point — usually our current position. Ending at $1,000 feels like a win if you started below it and a loss if you started above it.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: Why did scientists suspect the body made its own cannabis-like molecule?
choices: Because cannabis is a very common plant
Because the brain has a receptor that THC binds to, and the body wouldn't build a lock without a natural key
Because people reported feeling high without using cannabis
Because THC is found naturally in the human body
why: The discovery of a cannabinoid receptor implied a natural molecule meant to fit it — the body does not build receptors for a plant it may never encounter. That natural key turned out to be anandamide.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: What does the name 'anandamide' come from?
choices: A scientist named Ananda
The Greek word for brain
The Sanskrit word ananda, meaning bliss or joy
The chemical company that funded the study
why: Mechoulam's team named the molecule after the Sanskrit word ananda, meaning bliss — a nod to the cannabinoid receptor it binds to.

lessonTitle: The brain makes its own cannabis
q: Which statement best reflects a limitation noted in the discovery?
choices: Anandamide is extremely stable and easy to make into a drug
Anandamide breaks down within minutes and is only one of multiple endocannabinoids
Anandamide is the only molecule that binds the cannabinoid receptor
Anandamide proved cannabis has no effect on the brain
why: Anandamide is fragile and degrades quickly, and a second, more abundant endocannabinoid (2-AG) was later identified — so anandamide is one piece of a larger system, and turning the finding into stable medicine remains a work in progress.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: What was the ONLY major difference between the two groups of mice?
choices: One group ate fewer calories
One group ate a low-fat diet
One group could only eat within an 8-hour window
One group exercised more
why: Both groups ate the same high-fat food and the same number of calories. The single variable the researchers changed was timing: free grazing versus an 8-hour daily window.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: What does 'circadian rhythm' refer to?
choices: A type of low-calorie diet
The body's internal ~24-hour clock that governs processes like sleep and digestion
A heart-rate measurement
An exercise routine done at the same time daily
why: Circadian rhythm is your built-in roughly 24-hour clock. The study's big idea is that organs like the liver follow it, so eating at the wrong hours throws metabolism off.

lessonTitle: The mice that ate junk on a schedule and stayed lean
q: Why does the lesson urge caution about applying this to people?
choices: The mice were given a drug, not just a schedule
It was a mouse study, and a major human trial (TREAT, 2020) found the eating window alone gave no extra weight loss
The mice actually gained more weight
The study was never published
why: It was done in mice, whose metabolism differs from ours. Human results have been mixed — the 2020 TREAT trial found an 8-hour window alone wasn't more effective for weight loss than eating across the day.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: What was the key change the Transformer made versus older translation systems?
choices: It read text strictly one word at a time
It looked at all the words at once and weighed which mattered most
It used a much larger dictionary
It translated only between English and French
why: Older systems processed words in order. The Transformer's attention mechanism lets the model consider every word at the same time and decide which ones matter for understanding each one — which also let the work run in parallel.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: Why does 'in parallel' matter so much for training AI?
choices: It makes the model more polite
It lets many calculations run at once, fitting how modern AI chips work
It reduces the number of words the model needs
It guarantees a correct translation
why: Doing the work in parallel means many operations run simultaneously instead of single file. AI chips are built for exactly that, so the Transformer trained faster and could scale to far more data.

lessonTitle: The eight-page paper that built ChatGPT
q: What is the main 'catch' with attention?
choices: It only works in English
The cost grows with the square of the text length, so long inputs get expensive
It can't be sped up at all
It needs a human to label every word
why: Comparing every word to every other word means doubling the text roughly quadruples the work. That's why early models had short memories, and why making attention cheaper has been a major research focus ever since.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: In the study, who remembered more material a full week later?
choices: Students who re-read the passage
Students who tested themselves on it
Both groups remembered the same amount
Neither group remembered anything
why: After a week the self-testers held ~61% vs ~40% for re-readers — even though re-readers did slightly better on a test taken 5 minutes after studying.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: What is 'the testing effect'?
choices: Test anxiety lowering your score
Recalling information strengthens memory more than restudying it
Tests being unfair measures of knowledge
The boost you get from re-reading right before an exam
why: The testing effect is the finding that retrieving information from memory improves long-term retention more than passively reviewing it.

lessonTitle: Why testing yourself beats re-reading
q: Why do most people pick the less effective study method?
choices: It's faster
Re-reading feels fluent and confident while testing feels like struggling
Testing is more expensive
Teachers tell them to
why: Self-testers predicted they'd do worse, because retrieval feels effortful and exposes gaps — while re-reading feels smooth and reassuring. The comfortable method is the weaker one.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: What is the 'protein folding problem' AlphaFold tackled?
choices: Stopping proteins from breaking down
Predicting a protein's 3D shape from its amino-acid sequence
Counting how many proteins a cell makes
Translating DNA into amino acids
why: A protein's function comes from its folded 3D shape. Predicting that shape from just the order of amino acids — without measuring it in a lab — was a riddle biologists chased for about 50 years.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: How did AlphaFold use evolution as a clue to the shape?
choices: It evolved new proteins from scratch
It ignored other species entirely
Where two amino acids stayed in step across many related species, they're often touching in the folded shape
It measured how fast proteins mutate
why: AlphaFold gathers similar proteins from many species. Positions that changed together over evolution tend to be physically close in the 3D structure, which gives the model strong hints about how the chain folds.

lessonTitle: The AI that cracked a 50-year-old biology puzzle
q: What is a real limitation of AlphaFold's predictions?
choices: It only works on human proteins
Its shapes are guesses that need checking, and it's weak on floppy proteins and protein motion
It takes years to run
It can only predict one protein ever
why: AlphaFold outputs a confident predicted resting shape even when it should be uncertain, so results need validation. It's strongest on single well-folded proteins and weaker on disordered ones, on how proteins assemble, and on the motion they use to do their jobs.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: What physically changes in the brain during sleep, according to this study?
choices: Brain cells multiply rapidly
The gaps between brain cells widen, letting cleaning fluid flow through more freely
The brain shrinks permanently
Blood stops flowing to the brain
why: In sleeping mice, the interstitial space — the gaps between brain cells — widened by about 60 percent. That opened the channels so cerebrospinal fluid could surge through and rinse out waste much faster than while awake.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: Why does the beta-amyloid result connect this study to Alzheimer's disease?
choices: Beta-amyloid is the protein that clumps into plaques in Alzheimer's, and sleep cleared it about twice as fast
Beta-amyloid only exists in people who sleep well
The study cured Alzheimer's in mice
Beta-amyloid makes you sleepy
why: Beta-amyloid is the sticky waste protein that accumulates as plaques in Alzheimer's. Since the brain cleared it roughly twice as fast during sleep, weak or missing sleep could let it build up — a plausible, still-unproven link to brain decline.

lessonTitle: Your brain power-washes itself while you sleep
q: What is the main limitation a careful reader should keep in mind?
choices: The study was never published
The findings come from mice and haven't been fully confirmed in living humans
It only looked at one mouse
It proved sleep loss causes Alzheimer's
why: The detailed measurements were made in mice, and confirming the same fluid dynamics in living people is hard. The work shows clearance speeds up during sleep, but it does not prove that poor sleep causes Alzheimer's — that remains a hypothesis under investigation.

lessonTitle: Why the worst performers are the most confident
q: What did the lowest-scoring participants believe about their own performance?
choices: That they had failed badly
That they ranked well above average, near the 62nd percentile
That they ranked exactly in the middle
That they couldn't guess at all
why: People in the bottom quarter — about the 12th percentile on average — estimated they were around the 62nd percentile. The same lack of skill that caused them to do poorly also blocked them from seeing that they had done poorly.

lessonTitle: Why the worst performers are the most confident
q: According to the study, what eventually helped the worst performers judge themselves accurately?
choices: Being told to be more humble
Taking the test a second time
Being taught the actual skill they'd been tested on
Comparing themselves to comedians
why: In one study the researchers trained the bottom performers in the skill itself. Once they got better, they could finally recognize how poorly their first attempt had gone — self-awareness rose alongside competence.

lessonTitle: Why the worst performers are the most confident
q: What is one honest caveat about the Dunning-Kruger findings?
choices: The study was never actually published
Some of the effect is a statistical quirk where extreme guesses drift toward average
It only measured people who were already experts
The researchers made up the test scores
why: Part of the pattern comes from regression to the mean: guesses at any extreme tend to land closer to average, nudging low scorers up and high scorers down even without a true blind spot. How much is real missing self-insight versus this statistical pull is still debated.

lessonTitle: The trial that turned CBD into medicine
q: Why did the researchers use a placebo group instead of just giving everyone CBD and counting seizures?
choices: To save money on the drug
To make sure any improvement was due to CBD and not luck, hope, or the placebo effect
Because CBD is dangerous to give to everyone
To test two different doses of CBD
why: Parents had already reported fewer seizures, but those reports could reflect coincidence or the placebo effect. Comparing CBD against an identical fake pill — with no one knowing who got which — isolates the drug's real effect from wishful thinking and chance.

lessonTitle: The trial that turned CBD into medicine
q: What was the main result for the CBD group?
choices: Seizures were completely eliminated in everyone
The monthly convulsive seizure count was roughly cut in half, while the placebo group barely changed
CBD and placebo worked equally well
Seizures got worse on CBD
why: Median monthly convulsive seizures fell from about 12.4 to 5.9 on CBD — roughly half — versus a tiny drop from 14.9 to 14.1 on placebo. It was a meaningful reduction, though most children still had some seizures.

lessonTitle: The trial that turned CBD into medicine
q: What is a fair conclusion to draw from this trial?
choices: Any CBD gummy from a store will halve seizures
A purified CBD drug, at a tested dose, meaningfully reduced seizures in one severe epilepsy — but came with side effects and is not a cure
CBD cures all forms of epilepsy
CBD has no medical value
why: The trial tested one purified compound at a set dose for Dravet syndrome, showing real but partial benefit alongside side effects like drowsiness and liver-enzyme changes. It says little about unregulated retail CBD products, which were never tested this way.

lessonTitle: The math that made diversification a free lunch
q: According to Markowitz, what most determines a portfolio's overall risk?
choices: The expected return of the single best stock in it
How the holdings move relative to each other, not just how risky each one is alone
The total number of stocks you own
The fees you pay your broker
why: His core insight was covariance — how assets move together. Combining holdings that don't rise and fall in sync lets their swings partly cancel, lowering the whole portfolio's risk even if each piece is individually volatile.

lessonTitle: The math that made diversification a free lunch
q: Why is diversification often described as a 'free lunch'?
choices: It guarantees you will never lose money
Because assets don't move in perfect lockstep, blending them can lower risk without lowering expected return
It always increases your expected return
It removes the need to research any investments
why: Since assets move imperfectly together, a smart mix reduces overall swings while keeping the same expected return — a benefit you get without giving anything up. It lowers risk, but it does not eliminate it or guarantee gains.

lessonTitle: The math that made diversification a free lunch
q: What is a real limitation of using Markowitz's model in practice?
choices: It only works for government bonds
It needs estimates of future returns and correlations, which are noisy, and correlations can spike together in a crash
It ignores risk entirely
It requires you to hold exactly ten stocks
why: The optimizer depends on inputs estimated from past data, and small errors can swing the 'optimal' answer wildly. Worse, in a panic normally-uncorrelated assets often fall together, weakening the diversification just when it's needed.

lessonTitle: The day we heard two black holes collide
q: What is a gravitational wave?
choices: A beam of light from a distant star
A ripple in the fabric of spacetime caused by accelerating massive objects
A type of radio signal sent by black holes
An ocean wave caused by the Moon's gravity
why: Einstein predicted that when very massive objects accelerate hard — like two black holes spiraling together — they send ripples through spacetime itself, traveling at the speed of light. LIGO detected one of these ripples for the first time.

lessonTitle: The day we heard two black holes collide
q: Why did LIGO use two detectors thousands of kilometers apart?
choices: To make the experiment cheaper
To measure the wave's color
So a real wave from space shows up in both, while local disturbances usually hit only one
Because one detector wasn't powerful enough
why: A genuine gravitational wave passes through both detectors and registers in each. Local noise — a truck, a tremor, a footstep — typically affects just one site. Requiring the signal in both at once helps separate a real cosmic event from earthly jitters.

lessonTitle: The day we heard two black holes collide
q: Why is this detection called the start of a new kind of astronomy?
choices: It produced the sharpest photo of a black hole
It lets us observe events through spacetime vibrations, including ones that give off no light
It proved black holes don't exist
It replaced all telescopes
why: Traditional astronomy collects light. Gravitational-wave detection senses the vibration of spacetime instead, so we can observe violent events like merging black holes that emit little or no light — a genuinely new channel for studying the universe.

lessonTitle: The machine that taught itself to play Go
q: Why can't a computer win at Go simply by checking every possible game?
choices: The rules of Go are secret
There are more possible games than atoms in the universe
Go has no clear winner
Computers can't see the board
why: Go's branching is so vast — roughly 250 reasonable moves each turn over hundreds of turns — that the number of possible games dwarfs the number of atoms in the universe, so brute-force checking is impossible.

lessonTitle: The machine that taught itself to play Go
q: What were AlphaGo's two neural networks each responsible for?
choices: Drawing the board and keeping score
Suggesting promising moves and estimating who is winning
Translating the rules and timing the clock
Storing past games and printing results
why: The policy network proposed good candidate moves and the value network judged how favorable a position was — together they let AlphaGo focus its search on what mattered.

lessonTitle: The machine that taught itself to play Go
q: What milestone did AlphaGo reach in this 2016 paper?
choices: It solved Go completely
It beat a professional human champion on a full board, five games to zero
It replaced all human Go teachers
It learned Go with zero computing power
why: AlphaGo defeated European champion Fan Hui 5-0 in a formal match — the first time a program beat a professional human on a full-size board with no handicap.

lessonTitle: Breathing your way to a quieter immune system
q: What was the core claim the study tested?
choices: That cold water kills bacteria on the skin
That people can voluntarily influence their own immune (inflammatory) response
That meditation cures the flu
That young men have stronger immune systems
why: The point of contention was whether the innate immune response — long assumed to run automatically — could be consciously steered. The trained group showed it could be nudged, at least briefly, through a breathing-cold-meditation practice.

lessonTitle: Breathing your way to a quieter immune system
q: Why did the researchers inject everyone with endotoxin?
choices: To infect them with a real, dangerous disease
To boost their immune systems long-term
As a safe, controlled way to trigger a few hours of flu-like inflammation they could measure
To deliver the breathing training
why: Endotoxin is a fragment of dead bacteria the body reads as an infection. A tiny dose reliably and safely produces a short bout of flu-like symptoms, giving the team a consistent inflammatory challenge to compare across both groups.

lessonTitle: Breathing your way to a quieter immune system
q: What appears to be the main mechanism behind the calmer immune response?
choices: A surge in adrenaline (epinephrine) driven by the breathing technique
Colder body temperature freezing the bacteria
Extra sleep during the ten days
A change in diet
why: The breathing sessions drove epinephrine up sharply. That surge tracked with more of an anti-inflammatory signal and less of the inflammatory ones — pointing to the adrenaline spike, mostly from the breathing, as the likely engine.

lessonTitle: Why spreading out your study time beats cramming
q: What is the core finding of the spacing effect?
choices: Studying more total hours is the only thing that matters
The same study time produces more lasting memory when spread across separate sessions
Cramming the night before works best for long-term memory
Word lists are impossible to memorize
why: Holding the total amount of study fixed, breaking it into spaced sessions beat cramming it into one block — better later recall for the very same effort, across hundreds of experiments.

lessonTitle: Why spreading out your study time beats cramming
q: Why did the researchers run a meta-analysis instead of a single new experiment?
choices: Because they had no volunteers
To combine 100+ years of scattered experiments and see the overall pattern more reliably
To test a brand-new drug
Because single experiments are always wrong
why: A meta-analysis pools many past studies — here, 839 measurements from 317 experiments — so the overall pattern shows up far more clearly and reliably than any single study could reveal.

lessonTitle: Why spreading out your study time beats cramming
q: How should you choose the gap between study sessions?
choices: Always use exactly 24 hours, no matter what
The gap doesn't matter as long as you space at all
Make the gap longer the longer you need to remember the material
Shorter gaps are always better for long-term memory
why: The best gap scaled with the retention interval: to remember something for a week, space sessions a day or so apart; to hold it for months, stretch the gaps out to weeks.

lessonTitle: Finding the lock that THC fits
q: What did the researchers actually isolate and copy in this study?
choices: A new strain of cannabis plant
The gene (cDNA) for a brain receptor that responds to cannabinoids
A drug that blocks THC
A natural chemical the body makes
why: They cloned the cDNA — a working copy of the gene — for the cannabinoid receptor. That gave them the recipe for the 'lock' that THC fits, which is what made everything that followed possible.

lessonTitle: Finding the lock that THC fits
q: How did they prove the gene they found was really the cannabinoid receptor?
choices: They gave cannabis to volunteers and watched for a high
They inserted the gene into cells with no such receptor and showed those cells then responded to cannabinoids
They found it only in cannabis plants
They measured how much people smoked
why: Functional expression: put the cloned gene into cells that normally ignore cannabinoids. When those cells suddenly reacted — in a dose-dependent, stereoselective, chemistry-matching way — the gene had to encode the real receptor.

lessonTitle: Finding the lock that THC fits
q: Why was finding this receptor such a big deal for the field?
choices: It proved cannabis is harmless
It implied the body makes its own cannabinoid-like chemicals, opening up the endocannabinoid system
It replaced all cannabis research with computer models
It showed THC has no effect on the brain
why: If the body builds a lock, it likely makes its own keys. That logic led to the discovery of anandamide and the mapping of the endocannabinoid system — a control network behind pain, appetite, mood, and memory.

lessonTitle: Why the mug in your hand is suddenly worth more
q: What is the endowment effect, as this study measured it?
choices: People pay more for rare items
People value something more just because they own it, demanding more to sell than they'd pay to buy
People always trade goods to whoever values them most
People dislike making any kind of decision
why: Owners demanded roughly twice what buyers would pay for the identical mug. Simply owning it raised its perceived value — the gap between willingness-to-accept and willingness-to-pay is the endowment effect.

lessonTitle: Why the mug in your hand is suddenly worth more
q: Why did they also run a market using plain cash-value tokens?
choices: To make the experiment longer
As a check: tokens traded at the volume theory predicts, showing the mug undertrading wasn't just hassle or confusion
To prove tokens are worth more than mugs
Because students preferred tokens
why: Tokens have no personal attachment, so they should trade freely — and they did, matching the theory. That ruled out ordinary transaction costs and pinned the mug result on the endowment effect itself.

lessonTitle: Why the mug in your hand is suddenly worth more
q: What deeper psychological pattern best explains the endowment effect?
choices: Greed for money
Loss aversion — giving up what you own registers as a loss, and losses weigh more than equal gains
A preference for coffee
Random guessing under pressure
why: Once the mug is yours, selling it feels like a loss. Because losses loom larger than equivalent gains, owners hold out for a higher price — the core insight that helped build behavioral economics.

lessonTitle: The one-letter swap that made mRNA vaccines possible
q: Why did synthetic mRNA fail as a medicine before this work?
choices: It was too expensive to make
The immune system attacked it, triggering inflammation
Cells could not read it at all
It only worked in plants
why: Plain lab-made RNA tripped the body's Toll-like receptors, setting off an inflammatory response. That backlash, not cost or readability, was the wall blocking mRNA from being used as a drug.

lessonTitle: The one-letter swap that made mRNA vaccines possible
q: What was the core fix Karikó and Weissman found?
choices: Wrapping the RNA in fat bubbles
Replacing one of RNA's chemical letters with a naturally modified version like pseudouridine
Making the RNA much longer
Heating the RNA before injecting it
why: They swapped ordinary nucleosides for modified ones. RNA carrying these tweaks — pseudouridine especially — no longer set off the TLR tripwires, so the immune system left it alone.

lessonTitle: The one-letter swap that made mRNA vaccines possible
q: Why is it fair to call the fix only the first step toward a working vaccine?
choices: The fix was later proven wrong
Delivery, dosing, and stability — like lipid nanoparticles — had to be solved before it reached patients
It only worked against the flu
Vaccines never actually used this chemistry
why: Calming the immune alarm was essential but not sufficient. Getting protein production up and ferrying the fragile mRNA into cells with lipid nanoparticles came in later years, and that combination is what made real vaccines possible.

lessonTitle: The afternoon a contest entry kicked off the AI era
q: What made AlexNet's 2012 result so shocking?
choices: It was the first neural network ever built
It beat every other entry in the image contest by a large margin
It could write text and recognize speech too
It ran without any computer at all
why: In a contest where teams usually fought over fractions of a percent, AlexNet beat the next best system by about ten points. That lopsided win, not its novelty, is what stunned the field and signaled a shift.

lessonTitle: The afternoon a contest entry kicked off the AI era
q: Which combination made AlexNet work?
choices: Hand-written rules and a single photo
A deep neural network, a huge labeled dataset, and fast graphics chips
A faster internet connection
A brand-new idea no one had tried before
why: The pieces were a deep convolutional network, the million-plus labeled images of ImageNet, and gaming graphics cards fast enough to train it. The network design itself drew on ideas from the 1980s and 90s.

lessonTitle: The afternoon a contest entry kicked off the AI era
q: What is an honest limitation of AlexNet?
choices: It only worked for one day
It was a narrow specialist that named objects in photos and could learn the biases in its training images
It needed no data to train
It replaced all human jobs immediately
why: AlexNet could label objects in pictures and nothing else, and a network learns whatever patterns and gaps live in its training data — so it can be confidently wrong on unfamiliar images. Questions of reliability and fairness came later.

lessonTitle: Why reading on a screen before bed wrecks your sleep
q: What was the main biological effect of reading a light-emitting tablet before bed?
choices: It raised melatonin and deepened sleep
It suppressed melatonin by about half and delayed the body clock
It had no measurable effect on the body
It only affected eyesight, not sleep
why: On tablet nights, the sleep hormone melatonin was suppressed by roughly 50% versus paper-book nights, and the internal clock drifted about an hour and a half later — the core finding of the study.

lessonTitle: Why reading on a screen before bed wrecks your sleep
q: Why is the study's design considered strong despite its small size?
choices: It ran for ten years
It surveyed thousands of people online
Each person did both conditions in a controlled lab, serving as their own comparison
It used animals to avoid human variation
why: Twelve adults each spent five evenings on the tablet and five with a printed book under tightly controlled lab conditions. Having each person experience both setups means differences can be pinned on the screen, not on differences between people.

lessonTitle: Why reading on a screen before bed wrecks your sleep
q: What is the most honest limitation to keep in mind?
choices: The screens used were not actually electronic
Only twelve people read for four hours under artificial lab conditions, so real-world effects are likely milder
Melatonin has nothing to do with sleep
The study proved night mode does nothing
why: With just twelve participants and four full hours of bright reading before a fixed bedtime, the lab setup pushed the effect hard. Everyday use tends to be shorter and dimmer, so the direction holds but the size for any individual will vary.

lessonTitle: The real study behind the 10,000-hour rule
q: What did Ericsson and colleagues mean by 'deliberate practice'?
choices: Any time spent playing or performing
Focused, effortful work aimed at fixing weaknesses, usually alone and not very fun
Practicing only the pieces you already play well
Watching experts perform and taking notes
why: The whole point of the study was that the kind of practice matters. Deliberate practice is effortful, improvement-focused work that stretches you past your current ability — distinct from mindlessly repeating what you can already do.

lessonTitle: The real study behind the 10,000-hour rule
q: What did the practice totals look like across the three groups of violinists by age 18?
choices: All three groups had nearly identical hours
The teachers had the most, the best the fewest
The best had the most (~7,400 hours), then the good, then the future teachers
Only the best group kept any records
why: Accumulated deliberate practice lined up with skill: roughly 7,400 hours for the best by age 18, about 5,300 for the good group, and about 3,400 for the future music teachers — with the top group passing 10,000 hours a couple of years later.

lessonTitle: The real study behind the 10,000-hour rule
q: What is the most honest caveat about the '10,000-hour rule' the study inspired?
choices: Practice has no effect on skill
10,000 hours guarantees you'll become an expert
The number was an average for one group at one age, and later work finds practice explains only about a quarter of performance differences
The study was about athletes, not musicians
why: Ericsson himself rejected the '10,000-hour rule.' That figure was a group average, not a threshold, and a large 2019 reanalysis found deliberate practice accounts for only around 25% of the variation in performance — leaving room for genetics, starting age, coaching, and luck.

lessonTitle: The runner's high isn't an endorphin thing
q: Why did the researchers doubt the endorphin explanation before running the study?
choices: Endorphins don't exist in mice
Endorphins are large and don't easily cross from blood into the brain
Endorphins only affect muscles, never mood
Endorphins are destroyed by exercise
why: Endorphins are big molecules that struggle to cross the blood-brain barrier, so it was unclear how blood-borne endorphins could produce a brain-based high in the first place.

lessonTitle: The runner's high isn't an endorphin thing
q: What happened when running mice were given naloxone, an endorphin blocker?
choices: They stopped running entirely
Their anxiety got much worse
The post-run calm and pain relief stayed the same
They became more sensitive to pain immediately
why: Switching off the endorphin system changed nothing about the runner's high, which is the key clue that endorphins weren't producing it.

lessonTitle: The runner's high isn't an endorphin thing
q: What erased the runner's high in the mice?
choices: Blocking the endocannabinoid system
Feeding them more before the run
Letting them rest an extra hour
Lowering the room temperature
why: Blocking cannabinoid receptors removed both the calm and the pain relief, and mice lacking the CB1 receptor got no benefit at all, pointing straight at the endocannabinoid system.

lessonTitle: The stock market jumps around way too much
q: What did Shiller compute as his benchmark 'correct' price?
choices: The average price over the previous ten years
A price based on the dividends companies actually ended up paying
The price predicted by a computer trading model
The highest price each stock ever reached
why: He used a perfect-hindsight view: knowing every dividend that was actually paid, he discounted them back to find the one price a fully rational market should have set. That smooth line was his yardstick.

lessonTitle: The stock market jumps around way too much
q: What was the surprising relationship between the two lines?
choices: The real market price was far more volatile than the dividend-based 'correct' price
The two lines matched almost perfectly
Dividends were more volatile than prices
Prices were much smoother than the correct price
why: If investors were rational forecasters, prices should have been calmer than the dividends they were predicting. Instead the real market swung far more wildly, the heart of the 'excess volatility' puzzle.

lessonTitle: The stock market jumps around way too much
q: What's the main caveat critics raised about the finding?
choices: Shiller used made-up dividend data
Stocks don't actually pay dividends
If the discount rate itself swings, some extra volatility becomes more reasonable
The study only looked at a single company
why: Shiller assumed a steady discount rate. If that yardstick shifts as investors' appetite for risk changes, part of the 'too much' movement can be re-explained, which is why the exact size of the effect is still debated.

lessonTitle: The chip that did in minutes what a supercomputer couldn't
q: What was the point of the task Sycamore performed?
choices: To break a real encryption key
To design a new drug molecule
To complete a deliberately hard benchmark that classical computers struggle with
To run a faster web search
why: The random circuit sampling task had no practical use. It was chosen precisely because it is brutally hard for classical computers, making it a clean test of whether a quantum machine could pull ahead.

lessonTitle: The chip that did in minutes what a supercomputer couldn't
q: Why did IBM challenge the result?
choices: They proved Sycamore never ran at all
They argued a classical supercomputer with enough storage could do the job in about two and a half days, not 10,000 years
They showed qubits don't exist
They built a faster quantum chip the same week
why: IBM's objection was about the comparison, not the chip. With enough storage, they estimated a classical supercomputer needed only days, which dramatically shrinks the claimed gap.

lessonTitle: The chip that did in minutes what a supercomputer couldn't
q: What makes a qubit different from an ordinary bit?
choices: It is always exactly 0 or 1
It can only store text, not numbers
It can hold a blend of 0 and 1 at once until measured
It runs hotter than a normal bit
why: A classical bit is strictly 0 or 1. A qubit can exist in a blend of both states until it is measured, and with many qubits entangled together that lets the machine explore an enormous number of possibilities in parallel.

lessonTitle: The program that taught itself to play 49 Atari games
q: What information did the DQN agent use to learn each game?
choices: A written rulebook for every game
Only the screen pixels and the score
Expert human demonstrations of each game
The game's source code
why: The whole point was generality. The agent got no rules and no hints, only what a human player sees on screen plus the running score, and it had to figure out good play from that alone.

lessonTitle: The program that taught itself to play 49 Atari games
q: What made the result striking compared with earlier game-playing software?
choices: It was hand-tuned separately for each of the 49 games
The same single program and settings learned all 49 games
It only worked on one game, Breakout
It needed a human to take over during hard levels
why: Older programs were custom-built per game. Here a single architecture with the same settings learned dozens of very different games, which is why it pointed toward general-purpose learning.

lessonTitle: The program that taught itself to play 49 Atari games
q: Which limitation did the agent clearly show?
choices: It could not read the score on screen
It learned every game faster than a human
It struggled on games where rewards came long after the actions that earned them
It refused to play more than one game
why: Games like Montezuma's Revenge require long stretches of exploration before any reward appears. The agent learns from reward signals, so when the payoff is far from the action, it had a hard time connecting the two.

lessonTitle: The 20-year monkey study behind 'eat less, live longer'
q: What did caloric restriction mean in this study?
choices: Skipping meals a few days a week
Eating about 30% fewer calories while still getting full vitamins and minerals
Cutting out all carbohydrates
Eating only at night
why: The restricted monkeys got roughly 30% fewer calories but a complete supply of vitamins and minerals. The point was to remove energy without causing malnutrition, isolating the effect of eating less.

lessonTitle: The 20-year monkey study behind 'eat less, live longer'
q: What was the headline survival difference the researchers reported?
choices: Both groups survived at the same rate
The freely fed monkeys lived longer
About 80% of restricted monkeys survived age-related causes versus about 50% of freely fed ones
All the restricted monkeys outlived all the others
why: Counting deaths from age-related causes, roughly 80% of the calorie-restricted monkeys were alive at the reported point, against about 50% of the freely fed group, alongside lower rates of diabetes, cancer, and heart disease.

lessonTitle: The 20-year monkey study behind 'eat less, live longer'
q: Why is the result still debated?
choices: A parallel NIA study found no clear survival benefit, likely tied to what the control monkeys ate
The monkeys were later found not to exist
Caloric restriction was shown to shorten lifespan
The study lasted only a few weeks
why: A similar study at the National Institute on Aging saw no clear survival edge. The leading explanation is that Wisconsin's control monkeys ate a sugary diet freely and may have been overfed, so the comparison group, not just the diet, shaped the outcome.
